My job is not to choose a culture. It is to bridge cultural gaps between two of them.
In Brazil you learn to read between the lines. In Canada you learn to say the line.
Any diagnosis that does not account for power distance will read the silence as agreement.
I would rather set out the expectations in week one than defend them in month six.
We should take into consideration that this team has never been asked for its opinion before.
One could argue that our feedback culture is not weak. It is simply spoken in a different register.
More often than not, difficult feedback in our Brazilian teams is delivered indirectly.
It would appear that the silence in those sessions has more to do with hierarchy than with language.
For the most part, our Canadian colleagues expect the expectations to be set out in writing.
That said, I would be careful not to overgeneralize. There is more variation inside each country than between them.
It was the lack of psychological safety that caused the disengagement, not the workload.
What I found most challenging was navigating the difference in communication style.
It is the relational culture in Brazil that makes direct feedback harder to implement.
What this team needs is not another framework. It is a leader who sets out the expectations in week one.
I read your brief. You write that our teams are harder to read than the Brazilian ones. Are you not at risk of overgeneralizing about two entire countries?

I would be, if I were describing people. I am describing tendencies. More often than not, difficult feedback in our Brazilian teams is delivered indirectly, and there is more variation inside each country than between them.
Fair. Then explain the finding I disagree with. You say our last engagement session failed. Nobody objected to anything in that room.

That is the finding. It was the power distance in that room that produced the silence, not agreement. Two of your professors have run their faculties for twenty years. Any diagnosis that does not account for that will read silence as consent.
And your solution is a Brazilian culture program, translated into English, for a faculty of doctors who did not ask for one.

What this faculty needs is not a program. It is to be asked before it is told. So I will set out the expectations on day one, and then I have a question nobody here has asked you: what do you think the problem is?
It was the power distance that produced the silence, not agreement.
Any diagnosis that does not account for hierarchy will read the silence as agreement.
What this faculty needs is not a program. It is to be asked before it is told.
That said, I would be careful not to overgeneralize. My job is to bridge cultural gaps, not to rank them.

Danielle, it is Claire. I have read your cultural brief, and I want to talk about one sentence in it before anybody else does. Page two. Brazilians avoid conflict. That is the sentence. No hedge, no evidence, and a claim about two hundred million people. Here is what will happen if the faculty reads that. They will not argue with you. They will simply stop reading, and everything true in the other nine pages will go down with it. Now, the second thing, and this one matters more. You wrote that our last engagement session went well because nobody objected. Danielle, nobody objects in that room. Two of those professors have been running their faculties for twenty years, and they say no the way they have always said no: in writing, three days later, in three very careful paragraphs. So do not read that silence as agreement. What I want you to bring on Thursday is not a comparison of two countries. Bring me one observation with a number behind it, and bring the words that say it is a tendency and not a rule. That is the difference between a diagnosis they will fight with you about, and a document they will quietly put in a drawer.

Danielle, this is Marc. A note before tomorrow, and I would rather you hear it from me than learn it in the room. There is one sentence that will end that session early. It begins with, in Brazil we, and it ends with a rule. Say that, and this faculty will hear a stereotype and stop listening to your evidence, and I will not be able to get them back. That is not hostility. It is how doctors are trained: we attack the claim, not the person, and a claim without a hedge is simply a bad claim. The second thing you should know is that nobody will contradict you tomorrow. Not one person. And you will leave the room believing it went well. Then the disagreement will arrive on Friday, in an email, in three careful paragraphs, and it will be copied to your director. That is how we do it here, and I am telling you because nobody else will. So here is what I actually want. Set out the expectations on the first day, in writing, so that we are not guessing in November. And then ask me the question nobody in this organization has ever asked me. Not what I need. What I think the problem is.

What surprised me most was the directness of the feedback.
It was the academic directors who pushed back, three days later, in writing.
More often than not, difficult feedback in our Brazilian teams is delivered indirectly.
It would appear that the silence has more to do with hierarchy than with language.
Any diagnosis that does not account for power distance will read the silence as agreement.

What this faculty needs is not a program. It is to be asked before it is told.
That said, I would be careful not to overgeneralize. My job is to bridge cultural gaps, not to rank them.
